request.question: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?

title: Impatient (Anna Abreu song)
Dolly Sinatra
She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Just a Pretty Face?
Forever and for Always
Face Facts
On to Victory (album)
Never Trust a Pretty Face
The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell
Pretty Face
sentences: "Impatient" is a song by Finnish singer Anna Abreu from her third studio album, "Just a Pretty Face?
Abreu co-wrote the song with longtime collaborator Rauli Eskolin (known professionally as Rake), and Patric Sarin.
Eskolin also produced the song, while Sarin provided additional backing vocals.
"Impatient" is a Pop ballad.
The song was released on 9 November 2009 in Finland, as the album's second single.
Dolly Sinatra (born Natalina Maria Vittoria Garaventa; December 26, 1896 – January 6, 1977) was the mother of American singer Frank Sinatra.
She was born in Lumarzo (Province of Genoa), in northern Italy; she immigrated to the United States when she was two months old.
When she was a child, her pretty face earned her the nickname "Dolly".
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.
It was the fourth country single from her album "Up!
The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.
Twain had announced that "Nah!"
would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.
It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.
The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.
It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.
Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.
The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.
The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.
Just a Pretty Face?
is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.
The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
"Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up!
The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.
The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.
The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.
The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.
In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.
Face Facts is a 1980 album from glam rock band T. Roth and Another Pretty Face.
Bon Jovi drummer Tico Torres plays on the album.

On to Victory is the ninth studio album recorded by the English rock band Humble Pie and the first with a new lineup including vocalist and guitarist Steve Marriott, Drummer Jerry Shirley, vocalist and guitarist Bobby Tench from The Jeff Beck Group, and American bassist Anthony "Sooty" Jones.
They recorded "Fool for a Pretty Face", which Marriott had written earlier and the song proved good enough for them to secure a recording contract with Atco in 1980.
In UK their material was released by Jet Records, owned by the former Small Faces manager Don Arden.

The album peaked #60 on the "Billboard" 200 album chart "Fool for a Pretty Face" was released as a single and reached #58 on the US singles charts and a promotional tour followed as part of "The Rock'n Roll Marathon", supporting Ted Nugent and Aerosmith
Never Trust a Pretty Face is the third studio album by French singer Amanda Lear, released by the West German label Ariola Records in 1979.
The album included notable hit singles "The Sphinx" and "Fashion Pack (Studio 54)", and turned out a commercial and critical success.
"The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell" is a song written by David Bowie and Reeves Gabrels for the album "Hours" in 1999.
The first single release from the album in Australia and Japan, while the rest of the world got "Thursday's Child" as their first single.
The first appearance of the song was on the soundtrack of the film "Stigmata" in 1999.
It charted and peaked at No. 30 in Japan.
The song's title takes influence from the song "Your Pretty Face Is Going to Hell" by The Stooges from their album "Raw Power" produced by Bowie himself.
Pretty Face (プリティフェイス , Puriti Feisu ) is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Japanese author Yasuhiro Kano.

The story revolves around a high school boy who suffers an accident and having his face reconstructed in the form of his crush, attempts to impersonate a girl whom his crush mistakes for her twin sister.
A common theme in the series is the loving relationships the characters share for each other, whether they be between siblings, friends, or lovers as well as Karma.
It was first serialized in Jump Comic's "Weekly Shōnen Jump" by Shueisha between May 2002 and June 2003, finally coming to an end at chapter fifty-two.
For North America Viz Media acquired the rights to release an English translation of the series.

title: She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Forever and for Always

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?
hop1_docs: She's Not Just a Pretty Face | "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.  It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

Just a Pretty Face? | Just a Pretty Face?  is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.  The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
Not Just a Pretty Face (Rowan Atkinson album) | Not Just a Pretty Face is a comedy album by British comedian Rowan Atkinson.

Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces | Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces is the seventh studio album by British jazz-rock band If, released in 1974.  The band was now back on Capitol Records for U.S. distribution.
Always is Forever Tour | Always is Forever Tour was a concerts tour by American singer Diana Ross in 1999.

Always & Forever (Randy Travis album) | Always & Forever is the second studio album by country music singer Randy Travis.  It was released on April 4, 1987 by Warner Bros.  Records.  Released from this album were the singles "Too Gone Too Long", "I Won't Need You Anymore (Always and Forever)", "Forever and Ever, Amen" and "I Told You So", all of which reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts.

Always and Forever (Kostas Martakis song) | "Always and Forever" is a song and CD single by Greek singer Kostas Martakis.  It was Martakis's entry in a race to be the Greek representative for the Eurovision Song Contest 2008.

hop2_docs: Forever and for Always | "Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up! " (2002).  The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.  The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.  The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.  The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.  In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.

Shania Twain (album) | Shania Twain is the self-titled debut studio album by Shania Twain, released on April 20, 1993.  Although not a hit when originally released, Twain's subsequent successful albums gained her legions of fans, which generated more interest in "Shania Twain" causing it to be certified platinum in the US for shipments of one million copies.  None of the album's songs were included in Twain's "Greatest Hits" album, released in 2004.

When (Shania Twain song) | "When" is a song by Canadian singer Shania Twain.  It is the second UK release from her 1997 album "Come on Over".  The song was written by Mutt Lange and Shania Twain.  The song was originally released in the summer of 1998.  It was the only one of the twelve singles from "Come on Over" not released to the United States.  It was released to Canada however, as a pop single in February 2000.  The song was included in the Come on Over Tour and in a medley for the Up!  Tour.  Shania herself has stated that "When" is her favorite song off Come On Over" in a concert special.  "When" is also the theme song for the Japanese drama "Cheap Love".

She's Not Just a Pretty Face | "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.  It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

From This Moment On (Shania Twain song) | "From This Moment On" is a song by Canadian singer Shania Twain that appeared on the album, "Come On Over" (1997).  The song was written by Twain, and additional production and songwriting was performed by Robert John "Mutt" Lange.  "From This Moment On" has been performed on every tour by Twain since its release on March 14, 1998 in North America and Oceania.

Shoes (Shania Twain song) | "Shoes" is a song performed by Canadian singer Shania Twain.  It was the first single released from the "Desperate Housewives soundtrack" in 2005.  The song was written by Twain and then-husband, Mutt Lange, and co-written by Tammy Hyler, Joie Scott and Kim Tribble.  It is the last studio recording issued by Twain prior to the dissolution of her marriage to Lange, and the last Twain recording to feature any involvement from Lange.  The song was originally written and recorded for use on the ABC television program "Desperate Housewives".

When You Kiss Me | "When You Kiss Me" is a song by Canadian singer Shania Twain.  It was the fifth international single released from her 2002 album "Up! ".  The song was written by Mutt Lange and Shania Twain.  Shania has admitted on several occasions that "When You Kiss Me" is her favorite ballad off "Up!" ; in spite of that, it was excluded from Twain's "Greatest Hits" album.  The song had mild success in Europe and Australia; however, it was the only non-released track to chart on the "Billboard" Country Chart in the US.  A DVD release of "When You Kiss Me" marked Shania's first and only DVD single.  The song was also released as the last single to Canadian country radio.
